ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for fried wonton; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class. Specific fried wonton entry is absent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for fried wonton; not clinically coded as a distinct food entity in SNOMED CT hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried wonton in Singapore is approximately 4 pieces, weighing around 80 g, commonly served as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Wanton (Pork and Prawn) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Wanton (Pork and Prawn), CR ID F03060014. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried wonton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on the closest analogous food: deep-fried wheat-based dumpling wrappers with refined flour, consistent with GI values reported for fried wheat-flour snacks and dim sum items (GI range 65–80) in Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed publications. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 21.5) / 100 ≈ 15.5, using 80 g serving size providing approximately 21.5 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 735 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit fried wonton intake to no more than 2–3 pieces per occasion and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chilli sauce, as a single 80 g serving already delivers approximately 588 mg sodium, approaching the 600 mg per-meal sodium ceiling recommended for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from refined wheat wrapper combined with 26.9 g/100 g carbohydrate and deep-frying-induced fat load of 29.9 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit fried wonton because the refined wheat wrapper produces a high glycemic response (GI ~72), the deep-frying adds substantial fat that contributes to insulin resistance, and the low dietary fibre content (1.5 g/100 g) provides minimal glycemic buffering; steamed or boiled wonton is a significantly lower-risk alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 29.9 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g, combined with sodium 735 mg/100 g and deep-frying process generating oxidised lipids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of fried wonton due to its high total fat content (29.9 g/100 g), elevated saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g), very high sodium (735 mg/100 g), and the presence of oxidised lipids and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated during deep-frying, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles, arterial stiffness, and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g exceeds 5 g threshold; sodium 735 mg/100 g promotes tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried wonton as deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that amplify joint inflammation, the pork filling contributes arachidonic acid that drives inflammatory eicosanoid pathways, and the very high sodium content (735 mg/100 g) promotes fluid retention and tissue swelling around joints.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried wonton (pork and prawn) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean deep-fried snack consisting of thin wheat-flour wonton wrappers filled with a seasoned mixture of minced pork and prawn, then deep-fried in oil until golden and crispy. The exterior is light, shatteringly crisp, and blistered, while the interior filling is savoury, juicy, and slightly springy from the prawn. The dish is commonly served as a snack, appetiser, or side dish in hawker centres, coffee shops, and Chinese restaurants across Singapore, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or plum sauce for dipping. Nutritionally, fried wonton is energy-dense at 424 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 29.9 g/100 g resulting from deep-frying absorption into the wrapper, a moderate protein content of 11.7 g/100 g from the pork and prawn filling, and 26.9 g/100 g carbohydrate from the refined wheat wrapper. Sodium is very high at 735 mg/100 g due to soy sauce, oyster sauce, and salt used in the filling seasoning. Dietary fibre is low at 1.5 g/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high category) based on the refined wheat wrapper and deep-frying method. Clinically, this food is of high concern for hypertension (very high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, high fat, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat, sodium, and oxidised lipids from frying), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGE formation from deep-frying, arachidonic acid from pork). Steamed or boiled wonton is a substantially healthier preparation alternative for individuals managing any of these conditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried wonton?
Is fried wonton high in fat?
Fried wonton vs steamed wonton calories
Can I eat fried wonton with high blood pressure?
How much protein in fried wonton?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and secondary nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. CR ID F03060014 is an exact match. Saturated fat estimated at ~6.5 g/100 g based on typical pork-and-prawn deep-fried wonton composition from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous deep-fried pork-and-shrimp dumpling entries, as the HPB database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, sugar breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fried wonton preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI value of 72 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried refined wheat-flour snack/dumpling data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for fried wonton exists in approved sources. 4. The food_group 'Fish and seafood' is preserved from the HPB source classification despite the primary filling being pork and prawn; this reflects HPB's classification convention for seafood-containing dishes. 5. is_halal is set to false as the filling contains pork, making this food not permissible under halal dietary requirements.
